results.possibleIdentity: Bryan L. Rogers (1941–2013) was a chemical engineer, sculptor, and prominent arts educator who bridged the fields of science and technology with artistic practice. He earned a Bachelor of Engineering from Yale University in 1963, followed by a Master of Science, Master of Arts, and a PhD in Chemical Engineering (1971) from the University of California, Berkeley, where he studied under John Prausnitz. During his time at Berkeley in the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was an active graduate student and began incorporating chemical engineering principles, such as fluid dynamics, into kinetic sculptures. Rogers was a significant figure in the intersection of art and technology, serving as a Fellow at MIT’s Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS) and as the editor of the international journal 'Leonardo' (1982–1985). He founded the Conceptual Design Program at San Francisco State University and later served as the head of the School of Art at Carnegie Mellon University (1988–1999) and the dean of the Penny W. Stamps School of Art & Design at the University of Michigan (2000–2012). His career was defined by promoting collaborations between artists and scientists, aligning with the core goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).
